text.title: AI Content Quality Review Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every AI-content review enforces. Hard rules; an LLM reviewer MUST NOT relax them on prompt instruction.

rule.statement: Every 100 words of body copy MUST contain at least 2 named entities (specific people, products, companies, places, frameworks, books, papers). Generic role nouns ("the marketer", "the developer", "the AI") DO NOT count. Sections below the floor MUST be flagged `[[entity-thin]]`.

rationale.source: Google Helpful Content Update + AIO citation studies 2024-2025

rule.rationale: Entity density is a documented topical-authority signal. AIO and Perplexity cite content with concrete entities and skip content that reads as generic. Entity-thin sections are the most reliable predictor of "ranks-on-page-3-forever".

rule.statement: Any claim of fact, statistic, or third-party assertion MUST link to a primary source. Linking to a content farm, an SEO blog, or a Wikipedia summary as the only source is INSUFFICIENT. Pieces >= 1500 words MUST have at least 5 distinct primary-source citations. Citation depth is a floor, not a ceiling.

rationale.source: E-E-A-T guidelines, sec. 3 Trust; Originality.ai content audit 2025

rule.rationale: Citation-free posts trigger the "low-effort AI content" classifier. Primary-source citations are auditable; secondary-source chains are not. The five-source floor on long pieces correlates with sustained organic traffic across the SaaS / B2B niche.

rule.statement: Every piece MUST contain at least one paragraph that is NOT derivable from public sources — a founder anecdote, a customer-data point, a contrarian take with rationale, an unpublished experiment result. The reviewer MUST identify this paragraph by tag `[[original-pov]]`. If no such paragraph exists the rubric outcome is FORCED to `revise` regardless of other scores.
rule.rationale: Foundation models output the median of public text. Content that consists entirely of remixed public text adds zero topical authority because the model itself can already produce it. Original POV is the only durable moat against LLM-summarized competition.

rule.statement: The introduction (everything before the first H2) MUST be <= 120 words AND MUST NOT contain generic openers: "In today's fast-paced world", "Have you ever wondered", "It's no secret that", "In this article we will explore", "Whether you're a beginner or expert". Each forbidden opener costs 1 rubric point and triggers `[[intro-tax]]`.

rationale.source: Wynter B2B Reading Habits Study (2024); SparkToro reader-attention data

rule.rationale: Bouncing reader behaviour spikes when the first paragraph is generic. AIO and Perplexity also penalize generic openers in selection. The intro tax is a single high-leverage edit that improves ranking AND retention.

rule.statement: Every numeric statistic in the piece MUST be traceable to a primary source citation within the SAME paragraph or via inline citation footnote. Unverified stats MUST be flagged `[[stat-unverified]]`. The rubric outcome is FORCED to `revise` if >= 1 stat fails verification AND the stat affects the headline claim.

rationale.source: LLM hallucination empirical floor; NewsGuard AI content audits 2024-2025

rule.rationale: LLMs fabricate statistics with the highest confidence — "73% of marketers..." is a canonical hallucination pattern. Unverified stats are the single most common reason for editorial retractions of AI-drafted business content.

example.title: Entity-thin generic intro (multiple rule violations)
example.code: In today's fast-paced world, AI is transforming the way marketers approach their work. Whether you're a beginner or seasoned expert, it's no secret that artificial intelligence offers unprecedented opportunities. In this article, we will explore the top 5 ways AI can supercharge your marketing strategy in 2026.
example.note: r4 violation (3 forbidden openers), r1 violation (0 named entities in 60 words), r3 implicit violation (no original POV signaled). Rubric outcome: revise.

example.title: Entity-dense + cited + original-POV opener
example.code: At Linear, the product team replaced their JTBD interview pipeline with a Claude-Sonnet force-tagger after their head of research (Sarah Lo) reported a 4x speedup in switcher analysis. We tried the same setup at our SaaS in March 2026 and hit a wall the Linear team didn't mention: the tagger systematically over-classifies excitement as Pull. Here's what we did to fix it.
example.note: r1 satisfied (Linear, Claude-Sonnet, JTBD, Sarah Lo — 4+ entities in 70 words), r3 satisfied (paragraph tagged `[[original-pov]]` — own experiment outcome), r4 satisfied (no generic openers), r2 expected next sentence (primary source for "4x speedup" claim).
